data.title: Gildan Activewear Q1 Loss And Margin Hit Challenge Bullish Growth Narratives
data.description:
Gildan Activewear (TSX:GIL) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of US$1.2b and a basic EPS loss of US$0.30, setting up a quarter where the top line held up while profitability came under pressure. Over recent periods the company has seen revenue move from US$711.7m in Q1 2025 to US$1.2b in Q1 2026, while trailing twelve month basic EPS now sits at US$1.58 against a backdrop of a net profit margin of 6.2% over the last year versus 12.4% a year earlier. This shift puts the focus squarely on how much of each sales dollar is ultimately sticking as profit. Overall, these results leave investors weighing solid revenue scale against compressed margins and the impact of a large one off loss on recent earnings quality.

See our full analysis for Gildan Activewear.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this mix of revenue growth, weaker margins and one off items lines up against the prevailing bull and bear narratives around Gildan Activewear.

See what the community is saying about Gildan Activewear

Advertisement

Revenue climbs while profit swings to a US$55.1m loss

  • Total revenue in Q1 2026 came in at US$1.17b, up from US$711.7m in Q1 2025, but net income excluding extra items moved from a profit of US$84.7m a year ago to a loss of US$55.1m.
  • What stands out for the bullish camp is that this period of revenue expansion sits alongside forecasts of earnings growing about 25.6% per year and revenue around 12.9% per year. However, the trailing twelve month net profit margin sits at 6.2% compared with 12.4% a year earlier, which creates a tension between the growth story and the recent hit to profitability.
    • Bullish views around stronger margins and earnings growth are being tested by that halving of net margin in the trailing year and the Q1 swing from profit to loss.
    • At the same time, trailing twelve month revenue of about US$4.07b provides the scale that bullish investors reference when they argue that any recovery in margins could have a large impact on absolute earnings.
On top of that revenue scale, bulls point to longer term growth forecasts and margin recovery expectations as key reasons they think the business can move past this loss making quarter. They use detailed narrative work to spell out how that could play out in practice. 🐂 Gildan Activewear Bull Case

Margins hit by a US$211.3m one off loss

  • Over the last 12 months, net profit margin fell from 12.4% to 6.2%, and that period also includes a one off loss of US$211.3m that materially affected reported earnings.
  • Bears highlight that this combination of a large one time charge and weaker margins, together with debt that is not well covered by operating cash flow, supports a cautious stance even though the business still produced trailing twelve month net income of US$254.2m.
    • The margin compression from 12.4% to 6.2% gives bears a concrete data point when they argue that profitability in a largely basic apparel portfolio is under pressure.
    • The presence of a US$211.3m one off loss alongside concerns about debt coverage means skeptics can point to both income statement and balance sheet related risks when they question how durable those earnings are.
Skeptical investors often lean on this mix of thinner margins, a sizeable one off charge and leverage concerns to build out a more cautious narrative that focuses on potential downside if profitability does not stabilise. 🐻 Gildan Activewear Bear Case

Rich 44.8x P/E despite DCF fair value gap

  • The shares trade on a P/E of 44.8x, above both the North American Luxury industry average of 22.7x and a peer average of 38.3x, while the current price of CA$83.61 sits below a DCF fair value estimate of CA$138.41 and an analyst price target of CA$103.23.
  • Consensus narrative points to forecast revenue and earnings growth above market rates as a reason some investors accept a higher P/E. However, the combination of a 6.2% trailing net margin, the US$211.3m one off loss and a current price that is lower than both the DCF fair value and the analyst target means valuation signals are mixed rather than one sided.
    • The DCF fair value of CA$138.41 versus the CA$83.61 share price suggests upside on that model, which lines up with the idea of stronger medium term growth, while the 44.8x P/E reminds readers that the stock is already priced above industry and peer averages on current earnings.
    • The analyst price target of CA$103.23 also sits above the current share price, yet the deterioration in reported margin and the recent quarterly loss give context for why some investors may pause before relying only on those upside indicators.

data.title: FDA Proposal Could Support Novo Nordisk GLP‑1 Exclusivity And Valuation
data.description:
• The US FDA has proposed removing semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from federal compounding exemptions.
  • The proposal would limit pharmacies from compounding versions of these GLP-1 medicines when approved products are available.
  • This move directly affects Novo Nordisk’s GLP-1 portfolio, including its branded diabetes and obesity drugs under ticker NYSE:NVO.

Novo Nordisk, traded as NYSE:NVO, is a major player in diabetes and obesity treatments, with GLP-1 therapies at the center of its product lineup. The FDA’s proposal focuses on restricting compounded copies of GLP-1 drugs built on semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide, which are core ingredients in some of the company’s best known therapies.

For investors, the proposal is worth tracking because it relates to product exclusivity and protection of branded GLP-1 drugs. If the rulemaking advances, it could influence how much competition Novo Nordisk faces from compounded alternatives and how tightly its intellectual property and regulatory protections are enforced in the US market.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Novo Nordisk by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Novo Nordisk.

Is Novo Nordisk's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

The FDA’s proposal to remove semaglutide, tirzepatide, and liraglutide from federal compounding exemptions directly targets a source of lower cost, non‑branded competition to approved GLP‑1 products. For Novo Nordisk, which relies heavily on GLP‑1 drugs for diabetes and obesity, fewer compounded copies could help keep more prescription volume within its approved brands instead of flowing to pharmacies that mix their own versions from bulk ingredients. The agency also framed the move as support for the integrity of the drug approval process, which tends to favor companies that have invested heavily in clinical trials and regulatory work.

Operationally, the proposal may reduce pricing pressure from compounded alternatives that can undercut list prices, especially in self‑pay or under‑insured segments. However, it does not address other sources of competition such as biosimilars, potential price cuts, or rival GLP‑1 offerings from companies like Eli Lilly and Pfizer. Investors will likely focus on how quickly the proposal progresses, what final form the rule takes, and whether there are carve outs for specific clinical settings that still allow some compounding activity.

Advertisement

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ The company already faces pricing pressure in GLP‑1 therapies, and regulatory changes do not remove broader reimbursement or discounting risks.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have highlighted a high level of debt and dividends that are not fully covered by free cash flow, which can limit flexibility if competition intensifies.
  • 🎁 GLP‑1 drugs are a core earnings driver, and reduced competition from compounded versions could support the role of approved brands in this category.
  • 🎁 Simply Wall St’s checks indicate Novo Nordisk trades at good value compared with peers and the wider pharmaceuticals industry, while earnings grew by 1.4% over the past year.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on the FDA’s rulemaking timeline, including public comment and any revisions before a final decision. Watch how much compounded GLP‑1 volume actually shifts back to branded products and whether payers adjust coverage, prior authorization rules, or rebates in response. It is also worth tracking competitor moves, especially from Eli Lilly in GLP‑1 weight management, and any updates on pricing for Novo Nordisk’s US portfolio, given plans for lower list prices in 2027. Together, these factors will shape how much of the regulatory benefit translates into real world sales and cash flows for NYSE:NVO.

data.title: Here's What Analysts Are Forecasting For KONE Oyj (HEL:KNEBV) After Its First-Quarter Results
data.description:
Shareholders might have noticed that KONE Oyj (HEL:KNEBV) filed its first-quarter result this time last week. The early response was not positive, with shares down 6.8% to €54.20 in the past week. Revenues of €2.7b were in line with forecasts, although statutory earnings per share (EPS) came in below expectations at €0.41, missing estimates by 3.1%. Following the result, the analysts have updated their earnings model, and it would be good to know whether they think there's been a strong change in the company's prospects, or if it's business as usual. So we gathered the latest post-earnings forecasts to see what estimates suggest is in store for next year.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Following the latest results, KONE Oyj's 22 analysts are now forecasting revenues of €11.7b in 2026. This would be a modest 4.0% improvement in revenue compared to the last 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are predicted to climb 14% to €2.16. In the lead-up to this report, the analysts had been modelling revenues of €11.7b and earnings per share (EPS) of €2.16 in 2026. So it's pretty clear that, although the analysts have updated their estimates, there's been no major change in expectations for the business following the latest results.

View our latest analysis for KONE Oyj

It will come as no surprise then, to learn that the consensus price target is largely unchanged at €62.15. There's another way to think about price targets though, and that's to look at the range of price targets put forward by analysts, because a wide range of estimates could suggest a diverse view on possible outcomes for the business. There are some variant perceptions on KONE Oyj, with the most bullish analyst valuing it at €84.00 and the most bearish at €45.00 per share. This is a fairly broad spread of estimates, suggesting that analysts are forecasting a wide range of possible outcomes for the business.

Taking a look at the bigger picture now, one of the ways we can understand these forecasts is to see how they compare to both past performance and industry growth estimates. It's clear from the latest estimates that KONE Oyj's rate of growth is expected to accelerate meaningfully, with the forecast 5.4% annualised revenue growth to the end of 2026 noticeably faster than its historical growth of 1.9% p.a. over the past five years. Other similar companies in the industry (with analyst coverage) are also forecast to grow their revenue at 5.9% per year. KONE Oyj is expected to grow at about the same rate as its industry, so it's not clear that we can draw any conclusions from its growth relative to competitors.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The most important thing to take away is that there's been no major change in sentiment, with the analysts reconfirming that the business is performing in line with their previous earnings per share estimates. Happily, there were no real changes to revenue forecasts, with the business still expected to grow in line with the overall industry. The consensus price target held steady at €62.15, with the latest estimates not enough to have an impact on their price targets.

Keeping that in mind, we still think that the longer term trajectory of the business is much more important for investors to consider. We have estimates - from multiple KONE Oyj analysts - going out to 2028, and you can see them free on our platform here.

data.title: CTP (ENXTAM:CTPNV) One Off Gain Reshapes Earnings Narratives After Q1 2026 Results
data.description:
CTP (ENXTAM:CTPNV) has opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of €251.9 million and basic EPS of €0.44, setting the tone for how its latest results feed into the broader 12 month picture. The company has seen revenue move from €221.2 million and EPS of €0.41 in Q1 2025 to trailing 12 month revenue of €1.0 billion and EPS of €2.29. This gives investors a clearer sense of how the current quarter sits within its recent track record. With a large one off gain of €998.8 million still influencing the trailing numbers and margins softer than a year ago, this set of results leaves investors weighing headline growth drivers against the quality and sustainability of earnings.

See our full analysis for CTP.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how they line up with the key narratives around CTP's growth profile, risk factors, and earnings quality that have shaped expectations over the past year.

See what the community is saying about CTP

Advertisement

€998.8m one off still shaping trailing profit picture

  • Trailing 12 month net income sits at €1.1b, but this includes a €998.8m one off gain that makes underlying profit look stronger than it would from recurring operations alone.
  • Consensus narrative leans on multi year EPS growth of 11.5% a year and forecast earnings growth of about 6.2% a year. However, last year’s 4.5% earnings growth and softer margins highlight that a large part of the recent strength comes from that single gain rather than a steady improvement in profitability.
    • Revenue over the last 12 months is €1.0b versus €901.0m a year earlier, while net income over the same periods moves from €1.1b to €1.1b, which fits with the view that the top line has grown but profit momentum has cooled compared with the five year pace.
    • Analysts expect revenue growth of roughly 10% a year and earnings to reach €1.5b by around 2028. The reliance on a one off item in the latest figures is therefore an important contrast for anyone treating the trailing numbers as a clean base.

P/E of 7.1x against DCF fair value of €10.95

  • CTP is trading on a trailing P/E of 7.1x, below the peer average of 11.2x and the wider European real estate group at 13.2x, while a DCF fair value of €10.95 sits below the current share price of €16.08.
  • Consensus narrative points to analyst price targets of €21.10 and multi year earnings growth of 11.5% a year as reasons some investors see upside. The DCF fair value and more modest recent 4.5% earnings growth provide a different lens on what you are paying for today.
    • Against the current share price of €16.08, the €21.10 target implies room for upside based on analysts’ assumptions about 10.7% annual revenue growth and a 2028 P/E of 9.8x.
    • At the same time, the DCF fair value of €10.95 and shrinking profit margins compared with a year ago show why others might question how much of that long term growth is already reflected in the current valuation.

Interest coverage and dividend coverage sit on the weak side

  • The trailing figures indicate that interest payments are not well covered by earnings and the 3.98% dividend yield is not well covered by free cash flow, so both debt servicing and shareholder payouts draw heavily on current resources.
  • Bears focus on these coverage pressures as a key risk, and the data gives them some backing because weak interest cover and a dividend not supported by free cash flow could limit how comfortably CTP can fund its 2 million square meter development pipeline if conditions turn less supportive.
    • With €1.1b of trailing net income boosted by the €998.8m one off, the underlying capacity to meet interest costs looks thinner than headline profit might suggest.
    • The dividend yield of 3.98% comes alongside forecasts for 10% annual revenue growth, so anyone leaning on the bearish view will likely question whether cash should be kept inside the business rather than paid out if free cash flow remains tight.

Next Steps
